title: Acoustic regularities in infant-directed speech and song across cultures
methods: LASSO logistic classification with fieldsite-wise k-fold cross-validation
data_sources: The Music Lab 'Who's Listening?' citizen-science platform
core_conclusion: People worldwide reliably alter their speech and song when vocalizing to infants in consistent acoustic fashions, regardless of language or cultural background. LASSO classifiers identified infant-directedness from acoustic features alone with AUC 91% for speech and 82% for song across 21 diverse societies. In a citizen-science experiment, 51,065 listeners from 187 countries identified infant-directed vocalizations above chance even without shared linguistic relatedness to the vocalizer, confirming that these acoustic signals are universally intelligible. Speech and song show acoustically opposite patterns of infant-directedness (speech: more intense and contrasting; song: more subdued), implying distinct communicative functions rooted in evolutionary bioacoustics.
why_it_matters: Largest cross-cultural test of acoustic infant-directedness across 6 continents and 12 language families; reveals acoustic dissociation between infant-directed speech and song with distinct functional implications; demonstrates cross-cultural listener sensitivity via 495,512 citizen-science ratings; links human parentese to deep evolutionary bioacoustic principles; open audio corpus and reproducible code enable replication and extension.
why_nature: Global scope supporting a species-wide universality claim; multidisciplinary integration of field ethnography, acoustic signal processing, ML classification, and large-scale citizen science; evolutionary and developmental significance for human communication; the Music Lab citizen-science platform is a reusable methodological infrastructure; open-data and preregistration commitment meets NHB reproducibility standards.
added_value_to_my_research: GWR/MGWR applied to fieldsite-level acoustic effect sizes vs. urbanicity covariates — demonstrating spatial non-stationarity in behavioral data, not just built-environment outcomes

Crowdsourced audio data presents spatial sampling bias analogous to mobile-phone GPS trajectories; 2SFCA-based spatial weighting corrects for over-representation of urban internet users in citizen-science experiments
Explainable deep-audio models (SHAP/LRP on wav2vec embeddings) transfer the same attribution logic I use for spatial health-outcome predictions to the time-frequency plane
Inter-society contact networks structurally mirror intercity mobility graphs; my community detection and kernel-density catchment methods apply to mapping cultural acoustic convergence zones

Audio compression / bandwidth degradation experiments quantify a previously unmeasured developmental-health cost of digital access inequality, expressible as a 2SFCA accessibility gap for telehealth pediatric care

figures.caption: Cross-cultural regularities in infant-directed vocalizations: global map of 21 fieldsites and LASSO classifier performance across societies (speech AUC 91%, song AUC 82%) with top acoustic predictors

Acoustic feature differences between infant- and adult-directed vocalizations within voices: 11 significant features with fieldsite-level consistency (doughnut plots); speech and song show opposing acoustic trends
Naive listener sensitivity (d') across fieldsites, geographic distribution of 51,065 participants from 187 countries, and performance by linguistic relatedness between listener and vocalizer

LASSO models predicting naïve listener ratings from acoustic features: speech R²=0.59; song R²=0.18 — listeners' speech intuitions are more faithfully tuned to cross-culturally reliable acoustic cues than their song intuitions
